title: Why Your Cat Vomits Undigested Food After Eating | Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why cats regurgitate food immediately after meals, when it becomes a medical concern, and why a photo is a vital tool for your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat brings up food almost immediately after swallowing, it is often a process called regurgitation rather than true vomiting. Unlike vomiting, which involves rhythmic abdominal contractions and retching, regurgitation happens quickly and the material usually looks exactly like the food that was just consumed. This is most commonly caused by 'scarf and barf,' where a cat eats too quickly, causing the stomach or esophagus to reject the meal due to over-distension.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this behavior is typically Low, provided your cat is otherwise acting like themselves. If your cat is still energetic, drinking water, and using the litter box normally, you likely do not need an emergency visit. However, if this happens at every single meal, or if it is accompanied by lethargy, weight loss, or a refusal to eat, you should schedule a routine veterinary appointment to rule out food sensitivities or esophageal issues.

Why a photo helps triage

It may seem unpleasant, but taking a photo of the undigested food before you clean it up is one of the most helpful things you can do for your vet. A photo allows the medical team to see the shape and consistency of the material. For example, a tubular shape suggests the food never reached the stomach and was stuck in the esophagus, while the presence of bile or hairballs points to different underlying causes. This visual evidence helps the vet determine the best course of action during triage.

faqs.question: How can I stop my cat from eating too fast?
faqs.answer: You can use a slow-feeder bowl, a lick mat, or place a clean golf ball in the center of their dish to force them to eat around it, which slows down their consumption.

faqs.question: Could my cat have a food allergy?
faqs.answer: While less common for immediate regurgitation, chronic vomiting of undigested food can sometimes be linked to an intolerance or allergy to specific proteins.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about my cat throwing up?
faqs.answer: Seek veterinary care if the vomiting is frequent, contains blood, or if your cat stops eating, becomes lethargic, or shows signs of pain.
